title: Xenium Explorer Walkthrough of FFPE Human Breast Cancer
steps: This protocol is intented to serve as a quick orientation of 10X created Xenium Browser within their hosted FFPE Human Breast Cancer dataset. More information on this dataset can be found at https://www.10xgenomics.com/products/xenium-in-situ/human-breast-dataset-explorer and we will use it as a general guide for interrogating the dataset.

Upon introduction, you will see the tissue colored by clustered gene expression, similar to scRNA analyses prior to cell typing. Using the hosted gene lists, we will start coloring the cell based on transcripts of interest. We will start with DCIS cells, using markers provided. On the side bar, deselect Cells and select Transcripts. Within transcripts, enter in the genes of interest as included within the link above. From there, you can play with the various visualization methods. Below are some examples. The default "points" setting showing coloring of individual cells based on inclusion of genes of interest. Density plot showing cumulative expression of selected markers. Density plot showing expression on a per gene basis. You can access this visualization using the grid icon along the top.

Currently within the Xenium Browser, there is no way to save selected panels of genes as potential cell types. The browser does have predetermined clusters based on similar gene expression, so we will need to put in a little bit of extra work. Let's line up our density plot to the calculated clusters to see if we can start to infer cluster identities. To start, we will change plot settings to make the cluster colors pop. Below are plots showing the cells clustered by their cluster identity for the first 3 clusters. While cluster 2 looks like a different population then our DCIS, clusters 1 and 3 look pretty close to our density map when placed side by side. See below for a visualization of both clusters 1 and 3 highlighted. I continued to step through the clusters to see if any aligned with the DCIS cells as typed by our markers and ended up with the following. While we can't look at it directly here, it's interesting that so many different clusters correspond to DCIS. This could correspond to heterogeneity within the cell type and could be an avenue for further exploration.

Feel free to proceed with different cell types as included within the link above. While Xenium Browser lacks functionality for differential expression or saving groups based on cumulative transcript expression, this can be a jumping off point for further analysis outside of the browser. 10X recommends a number of tools to supplement and expand analysis capabilities, some of which are included below (taken from https://www.10xgenomics.com/analysis-guides/continuing-your-journey-after-xenium-analyzer ). Exploration of some of these tools may be the subject of later workshops.
creator: Cameron Baker
